hook: A death row killer believed to his last breath that he had a kind heart. Dale Carnegie spent twenty years collecting true stories like that on index cards, and what he found still explains why everyone you meet this week is, in their own mind, entirely reasonable.

sections.h: A Broke Salesman At The YMCA
p: In 1912 Dale Carnegie was broke, living in a cheap New York hotel and selling trucks for a living he hated. What he actually wanted to do was teach, so he talked his way into the YMCA on 125th Street in Harlem and convinced the director to let him teach a night class in public speaking, paid only by a cut of what the students gave him. There was no textbook, because nobody had written the one he needed yet.

One night he ran out of material halfway through class. With nothing prepared, he asked the man in the front row to stand and talk about something that had made him angry. The man did, and the room came alive. Carnegie had stumbled onto something enormous, that people do not need a script, they need permission to be honest. He kept at it for two decades, writing down every technique that worked on an index card, until an editor who saw one of his talks pushed him to turn the file cabinets into a book. That is why it reads like a few hundred people telling you what actually happened to them.

Carnegie opens with two of the most wanted criminals in American history. Francis Crowley, known as Two Gun Crowley, was cornered by a hundred and fifty police officers in Manhattan in 1931, and while bleeding behind a barricade he wrote of a weary heart, but a kind one, underneath his coat, certain to the end he was a decent man being hunted unfairly. Al Capone described himself the same way, a man who had given people harmless fun and was punished for it. Neither thought of himself as the villain of his own story, and Carnegie's point is that your spouse, coworker, and the driver who cut you off are doing the same thing on a smaller scale.

His favorite proof is Abraham Lincoln, who learned the lesson twice. As a young lawyer he wrote a mocking, anonymous letter that humiliated a man named James Shields, who challenged him to a duel with broadswords on a Mississippi sandbar, called off at the last minute. Lincoln never mocked anyone in print again. Decades later, after a flooded Potomac trapped Lee's retreating army following Gettysburg, General Meade hesitated and let Lee escape, dragging the war on two more years. Lincoln wrote him a furious letter but never sent it, and it was found folded among his papers after his death. Writing it let him blow off steam, without wounding a general who might have resigned at the worst possible moment.

The psychologist B.F. Skinner later found the same pattern in animals: reward makes behavior stick, punishment does not, it just breeds resentment that comes out sideways later. Department store magnate John Wanamaker put it simpler after thirty years in business: it was foolish to scold, since he had enough trouble with his own limitations without being upset that others had limitations too.
sections.pull: Everybody is the hero of their own story.

sections.h: Appreciation, And The Art Of Wanting
p: Carnegie is precise about the difference between appreciation and its cheap imitation, flattery. Flattery tells someone what they want to hear because you want something back. Appreciation notices something genuinely good in another person and says it out loud, expecting nothing. Charles Schwab, who ran Andrew Carnegie's steel operation and became one of the highest paid executives in America, credited his fortune not to knowing steel but to his ability to arouse enthusiasm in people, calling himself hearty in his approbation and lavish in his praise. John D. Rockefeller Senior showed the same instinct when a partner lost the company a million dollars on a bad investment. Rather than scold him, Rockefeller praised the sixty percent he had managed to save, and called it splendid.

The companion idea is to stop thinking about what you want and start finding what the other person wants. Carnegie liked strawberries and cream, he said, but fish prefer worms, so when fishing he baited the hook with what the fish wanted. As a boy, Andrew Carnegie caught a mother rabbit and soon had a hutch full of hungry babies with no way to feed them, so he offered every neighborhood boy a rabbit named after him if they brought in enough clover. Every boy showed up with armfuls of it. Owen D. Young of General Electric put the grown up version simply: a person who can put himself in another's place never has to worry about the future.
sections.pull: Nothing kills a person's ambition faster than criticism from above.

sections.h: How To Make People Like You
p: The first way is to become genuinely interested in other people. A dog does not have to sell you anything to make you love it, Carnegie points out, it just has to be thrilled you walked through the door. Theodore Roosevelt had that gift. His valet James Amos described Roosevelt returning for a visit years after leaving the White House and greeting every household staffer by name, asking the kitchen maid if she still made her corn bread, disappointed the family upstairs no longer ate it.

The second way is disarmingly simple: smile. Carnegie quotes an old Chinese proverb that a man without a smiling face must not open a shop, and cites a New York department store manager who said she would rather hire a shop girl with a genuine smile than a philosophy professor with a sour face. The psychologist William James observed that action and feeling travel together: act as though cheerfulness is already there, and the feeling tends to follow the muscle.

The third way is to remember that a person's name is, to that person, the sweetest sound in any language. Jim Farley, who ran Franklin Roosevelt's campaigns, could greet fifty thousand people by their first names, a skill built over years of filing away names, families, and jobs. Andrew Carnegie understood the same power: wanting the Pennsylvania Railroad's business, he built a steel works and simply named it after the railroad's president, and won the contract. Later, locked in a price war with George Pullman over a sleeping car contract, Carnegie proposed a merger and offered to call it the Pullman Palace Car Company. Pullman's face changed, and Carnegie walked away with half the business, for the price of a name on a door.

sections.h: Listen, Then Make Them Feel Important
p: The fourth way is to become a genuinely good listener. At a New York dinner party, Carnegie met a botanist and spent hours asking question after question about exotic plants, barely saying a word about himself all evening. When he left, the botanist told the host that Carnegie was one of the most stimulating conversationalists he had met in years. Carnegie's own verdict was that he had simply listened.

The fifth way is to talk in terms of the other person's interests. Roosevelt's staff noticed that whenever he knew someone was coming to see him, he stayed up the night before reading about whatever that visitor cared most about, cattle ranching, naval history, rare birds, so he could walk in ready to talk about their world instead of his own.

The sixth way, which Carnegie treats almost as the summary of everything before it, is to make the other person feel important, and mean it. He tells a story on himself: waiting behind a bored registry clerk at a post office, he said, genuinely, that he wished he had the clerk's head of hair. The young man looked up, startled, and broke into a real smile, a little embarrassed, but clearly delighted someone had noticed him at all that day. It cost Carnegie four seconds. Nearly everyone you pass today carries some quiet competence nobody has mentioned in a while.

sections.h: Avoid The Argument, Win The Person
p: The only way to get the best of an argument, Carnegie says, is to avoid it entirely. At a banquet, another guest misquoted a line from Hamlet as scripture. Carnegie knew better and turned to his old friend Frank Gammond, a Shakespeare scholar, for backup, ready to correct the man in front of the table. Instead Gammond kicked him under the table, and afterward told him to always avoid the acute angle. Nobody had asked for a correction, and proving a man wrong in front of a room was never going to make him like you.

The same discipline applies when you are wrong: admit it at once and fully. Carnegie used to walk his dog Rex off leash in a park, against the rules, and when a policeman caught him a second time, he spoke first, calling himself guilty, no excuses. The officer softened and let them go. From there, get the other person agreeing with you early, what Carnegie calls the secret of Socrates. A bank teller named James Eberson got a reluctant customer to fill out a form just by asking a few questions the man could only answer yes to. A Westinghouse salesman named Joseph Allison, after thirteen years failing to sell one customer a motor, defused a complaint that it ran too hot by agreeing first, then walking the man through the industry standard until he saw for himself the motor was fine, and placed a bigger order.

Better still, let the other person feel the idea was theirs. An illustrator named Eugene Wesson had failed for months to sell finished sketches, so he switched to bringing in unfinished ones, asking buyers how they thought the pieces should be completed. He could not stop selling after that, because the buyers now felt like part owners of the work. When reasoning alone will not land, dramatize it. Touring an underperforming steel mill, Schwab asked the day shift foreman how many heats, meaning melts of steel, his crew had finished that day. Six, the man said. Without a word of criticism, Schwab chalked a big six on the floor and walked away. The night shift, stung, rubbed it out and chalked a seven. The day shift saw it next morning and chalked a ten. The mill was soon outproducing every other plant in the company, because Schwab had turned a number into a contest nobody wanted to lose.

Begin every difficult conversation with honest praise and appreciation, because criticism that opens a conversation puts a person on the defensive before you have made your point. Then correct mistakes indirectly rather than head on. Walking through one of his mills, Schwab passed men smoking directly under a No Smoking sign. He did not yell. He handed each man a cigar and said he would appreciate it if they smoked outdoors instead. Every man there knew exactly what he meant, and respected him more for it.

Ask questions instead of issuing direct orders. Owen D. Young almost never told a subordinate flatly what to do. He would say, you might consider this, or, what do you think of that, which turns a person into a collaborator instead of someone just taking orders. When you need to make a real change, protect the person's dignity. General Electric once needed to remove the brilliant but difficult engineer Charles Steinmetz from a department he was not suited to manage, without insulting a man the company considered irreplaceable, so it invented a new title for him, Consulting Engineer of General Electric, and installed someone else as department head. Steinmetz was pleased, and nobody had to lose face.

Finally, give people, especially the ones you are hardest on, a fine reputation to live up to. A fourth grade teacher who learned she was getting a notoriously difficult student named Tommy told him, on his first day, that she had heard he was a natural leader and was counting on him to help make the class the best in the grade. Tommy, never once called a leader by an adult, spent the rest of the year quietly trying to live up to that description. It is the same instinct behind nearly every story in this book: treat a person like they already matter, and, more often than expected, they grow into it. Writers from Robert Cialdini to Chris Voss have spent decades rediscovering pieces of what Carnegie wrote on index cards in the 1930s.

takeaways: Go one full day without criticizing, condemning, or complaining about anyone, out loud or silently. When you catch yourself, ask what story that person is probably telling themselves.
Give one person you see almost every day one honest, specific sentence of appreciation this week, not a generic compliment but the exact thing you noticed.
In your next conversation with someone you need something from, spend the first few minutes finding out what they want before you mention what you want.
Pick one person whose name you have been getting wrong, forgetting, or avoiding, and use their full name warmly the next time you see them.
The next time you feel an argument building, ask whether winning it is worth what it will cost you. If you are the one in the wrong, admit it at once, before you are accused.
Take one instruction you were about to hand someone as a flat order and rephrase it as a question instead, such as what do you think about trying it this way.
Find one person, maybe a kid or someone new on your team, who could use a fine reputation to live up to, and give them one, sincerely, even if it is a little aspirational.
